source_quote: This directly conflicts with objective prioritization, as it prevents the rational assessment of alternative options or critical, disconfirming evidence.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'The tendency to prioritize or seek out information that confirms existing beliefs. This directly conflicts with objective prioritization.'
activation_condition: When existing beliefs or hypotheses bias the selection of priorities over objective assessment of alternatives.

source_description: Einstellung-effect distorts prioritization by making the most familiar framework feel like the most applicable one — solutions that fit existing tools rise in the priority queue regardless of actual fit.
source_quote: Maslow's Hammer / Availability Heuristic: This bias occurs when a person is over-reliant on a single mental model or framework, causing them to 'torture reality so that it fits your models'. This is dangerous because it leads to prioritizing solutions that fit the existing toolset.
affinity_rationale: Canonical (as Maslow's Hammer): 'over-reliant on a single mental model, causing them to torture reality so that it fits your models' — familiar tools override proper prioritization.
activation_condition: When familiar frameworks are applied to prioritize solutions rather than selecting the framework appropriate for the problem.

source_description: Prioritization conflicts with optionality when aggressive prioritization forecloses options that would have been valuable under high uncertainty.
source_quote: prioritization conflicts with optionality (optionality) when aggressive prioritization forecloses options that would have been valuable under high uncertainty.

source_description: Prioritization conflicts with simplification when priority ranking oversimplifies trade-offs between incommensurable objectives that resist ordinal ordering.
source_quote: prioritization conflicts with simplification (simplification) when priority ranking oversimplifies trade-offs between incommensurable objectives that resist ordinal ordering.

source_description: Incentives helps prisoners-dilemma reveal when individually rational behavior is being produced by a payoff structure that rewards defection.
source_quote: The model helps separate bad actors from bad rules by making the payoff structure explicit.
affinity_rationale: Incentive structures are central to PD dynamics — the payoff matrix IS an incentive structure. Canonical: outcome 'changes drastically' depending on how incentives frame cooperation vs defection.
activation_condition: When analyzing how payoff incentives drive cooperative or defective behavior in strategic interdependence scenarios.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps prisoners-dilemma diagnose bad collective outcomes as the product of a flawed interaction structure rather than isolated bad intent.
source_quote: It serves as a diagnostic tool in systems thinking, helping users recognize archetypes of common system structures that produce characteristic patterns of behavior.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'The PD structure is itself a common system archetype. Recognizing this archetype helps diagnose complex organizational issues' — structural pattern recognition.
activation_condition: When recognizing prisoner's dilemma patterns as systemic archetypes within organizational competition and collaboration dynamics.

source_description: Confirmation-bias worsens prisoners-dilemma dynamics when ambiguous moves are read as confirming that the other side is defecting.
source_quote: prisoners-dilemma conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when players interpret ambiguous moves to confirm that the other party is defecting
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Focusing solely on the competitive frame (betrayal as dominant strategy) without exploring alternative cooperative structures' when initial hypothesis is ingrained.
activation_condition: When players interpret ambiguous moves to confirm defection assumptions, accelerating mutual betrayal even when cooperation was intended.

source_description: Prisoners-dilemma conflicts with active-listening when incentive structures reward concealment over transparent coordination.
source_quote: prisoners-dilemma conflicts with active-listening (active listening) when incentive structures reward concealment over transparent coordination.

source_description: Prisoners-dilemma conflicts with empathy when defensive payoff framing overrides perspective-taking and trust repair.
source_quote: prisoners-dilemma conflicts with empathy (empathy) when defensive payoff framing overrides perspective-taking and trust repair.

source_description: Prisoners-dilemma conflicts with confirmation-bias when players interpret ambiguous moves to confirm that the other party is defecting, accelerating mutual defection even when cooperation was intended.
source_quote: prisoners-dilemma vs confirmation-bias: prisoners-dilemma conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when players interpret ambiguous moves to confirm that the other party is defecting, accelerating mutual defection even when cooperation was intended.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps probabilistic-thinking map the interdependencies first so the probability frame reflects the real system rather than isolated variables.
source_quote: When facing highly complex systems (e.g., banking or climate), one must first apply systems thinking to understand the totality and subtle interrelationships of components before creating a probabilistic framework.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'PT is often nested within a systems perspective. When facing highly complex systems, one must first apply systems thinking' — prerequisite framework.
activation_condition: When complex system interrelationships must be understood before constructing a probabilistic framework.

source_description: Expected-value gives probabilistic-thinking an operational way to compare choices with mixed upside and downside instead of hunting for certainty.
source_quote: Expected Value Calculations
affinity_rationale: Expected value IS a core probabilistic calculation — fundamental mathematical tool for weighing outcomes by their probabilities. Intrinsic relationship.
activation_condition: When decisions require weighting outcomes by their probabilities to determine optimal expected returns.

source_description: Falsifiability keeps probabilistic-thinking honest by forcing the team to define what evidence would actually resolve the uncertain claim.
source_quote: Strong hypotheses must be framed clearly so they are easier to challenge and pressure test.
affinity_rationale: Canonical (via HDPS): 'Strong hypotheses must be framed clearly so they are easier to challenge and pressure test' — falsifiability enables probabilistic hypothesis testing.
activation_condition: When probabilistic estimates require testable, falsifiable hypotheses to validate or refine probability assignments.

source_description: Confirmation-bias corrupts probabilistic-thinking by treating belief defense as if it were evidence weighting.
source_quote: PT, conversely, requires intellectual humility to actively identify and reconcile disconfirming evidence.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'This bias represents a failure of critical thinking, where people seek information that confirms existing beliefs. PT requires intellectual humility to identify disconfirming evidence.'
activation_condition: When existing beliefs distort probability estimates by selectively seeking confirming rather than disconfirming evidence.

source_description: Representativeness-bias can make probabilistic-thinking judge likelihood by resemblance to a familiar story instead of by the actual odds.
source_quote: The conflict is illustrated by the representativeness heuristic, where probabilities are incorrectly judged based on resemblance to a stereotype rather than mathematical likelihood.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'probabilities are incorrectly judged based on resemblance to a stereotype rather than mathematical likelihood' — System 1 substitution that defeats probabilistic reasoning.
activation_condition: When resemblance-based intuitions override proper base-rate calculations and statistical reasoning.

source_description: Probabilistic-thinking conflicts with narratives when story coherence makes a scenario feel more probable than its base rate warrants, overriding distributional reasoning.
source_quote: probabilistic-thinking conflicts with narratives (narratives) when story coherence makes a scenario feel more probable than its base rate warrants, overriding distributional reasoning.

source_description: Probabilistic-thinking conflicts with simplification when reducing uncertainty to a single point estimate discards the distribution information that probabilistic thinking requires.
source_quote: probabilistic-thinking conflicts with simplification (simplification) when reducing uncertainty to a single point estimate discards the distribution information that probabilistic thinking requires.

source_description: Metacognitive-questioning gives problem-framing-and-reframing the self-inspection needed to notice when the current lens is producing weak answers.
source_quote: When approaching a problem, expert practitioners often start by reflecting on how the problem is framed, engaging in a form of metacognition.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Framing requires thinking about thinking (metacognition)' — planning and monitoring aspects enable effective frame selection and evaluation.
activation_condition: When selecting or evaluating a problem frame requires reflective awareness of one's own reasoning process and assumptions.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps reframing move from surface symptoms to better boundaries, interdependencies, and underlying structure.
source_quote: Framing helps systems thinkers see the entire picture, searching for interdependencies and focusing on structure rather than fault.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Locates causal structure — framing helps systems thinkers see the entire picture, searching for interdependencies and focusing on structure rather than fault.'
activation_condition: When framing a problem requires mapping interdependencies and understanding how components interact within the larger system.

source_description: Anchoring is a useful ally only in reverse pressure here: it marks why reframing must generate alternatives before the first lens hardens into the answer.
source_quote: They should be generated separately from solutions to prevent anchoring.
affinity_rationale: Moderate: frames inherently set cognitive anchors that guide subsequent analysis. Canonical mentions HMW questions and reference frames as structuring tools.
activation_condition: When an initial reference frame is needed to structure problem exploration, provided it remains open to reframing.

source_description: Confirmation-bias resists reframing by defending the initial problem statement even after disconfirming evidence suggests a better cut.
source_quote: Confirmation bias drives problem solvers to seek evidence that confirms their initial framing or hypothesis.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Resists reframing: Confirmation bias drives problem solvers to seek evidence that confirms their initial framing, making them unwilling to investigate deeply enough to justify a reframing.'
activation_condition: When existing beliefs lock the problem frame in place and prevent exploring alternative framings despite disconfirming evidence.

source_description: Anchoring becomes an antagonist when the first framing or salient cue keeps the team from testing alternative boundaries and causal structures.
source_quote: Anchoring and Substitution Bias: Focusing too much on a single, salient piece of information (the anchor).
affinity_rationale: Canonical risks: 'Focusing too much on a single, salient piece of information (the anchor)' — root cause of the hammer-and-nail problem in framing.
activation_condition: When an initial frame anchors subsequent analysis so strongly that alternative frames are never seriously tested.

source_description: Representativeness-heuristic locks in the first pattern-matched frame by making it feel like the right one — the familiar structure activates fast and feels complete, which actively blocks the reframing process needed to find better causal boundaries.
source_quote: problem-framing-and-reframing vs representativeness-heuristic: problem-framing-and-reframing conflicts with representativeness-heuristic (representativeness heuristic) when first familiar frame blocks alternative causal structures.
affinity_rationale: Canonical tension: 'first familiar frame blocks alternative causal structures' — pattern matching overrides proper frame selection.
activation_condition: When a problem is classified by surface resemblance to a familiar type, blocking exploration of its unique causal structure.

source_description: Problem-framing-and-reframing conflicts with confirmation-bias when reframing is constrained to preferred hypotheses.
source_quote: problem-framing-and-reframing vs confirmation-bias: problem-framing-and-reframing conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when reframing is constrained to preferred hypotheses.

source_description: Problem-framing-and-reframing conflicts with representativeness-heuristic when the first familiar frame — activated by pattern resemblance — blocks alternative causal structures that reframing would otherwise reveal.
source_quote: problem-framing-and-reframing vs representativeness-heuristic: problem-framing-and-reframing conflicts with representativeness-heuristic (representativeness heuristic) when first familiar frame blocks alternative causal structures.

source_description: Anchoring helps explain why reference points and initial frames distort how prospect-theory choices are evaluated.
source_quote: This phenomenon also interacts with cognitive biases like Anchoring.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'anchoring bias occurs in the evaluation of complex, disjunctive events' — anchoring is one mechanism within the PT framework for reference-dependent evaluation.
activation_condition: When reference points anchor the evaluation of gains and losses in prospect-theoretic decision scenarios.

source_description: Incentives interact with prospect-theory because gain and loss framing can change behavior without changing the underlying economics.
source_quote: framing outcomes around relative gains or losses (emotional incentives)
affinity_rationale: Moderate: PT describes how loss aversion and reference dependence affect incentive design. Understanding PT is necessary for designing effective behavioral incentives.
activation_condition: When incentive structures must account for loss aversion and reference-dependent evaluation of outcomes.

source_description: Critical-thinking counters prospect-theory distortions by forcing a colder review of whether the frame is changing the choice more than the fundamentals are.
source_quote: not a plausible normative decision theory
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'PT stands against the notion that implementation or persuasion is a fundamentally rational process solved via logical, step-by-step methods' — hot cognition vs cold cognition.
activation_condition: When deliberate rational analysis would override the emotional and reference-dependent heuristics that PT describes as driving actual behavior.

source_description: Prospect-theory conflicts with base-rates when loss-aversion framing distorts assessment of objective frequencies by overweighting vivid potential losses.
source_quote: prospect-theory conflicts with base-rates (base rates) when loss-aversion framing distorts assessment of objective frequencies by overweighting vivid potential losses.

source_description: Prospect-theory conflicts with anchoring when reference-point dependence means the same objective outcome is evaluated differently depending on the arbitrary anchor that sets the gain/loss boundary.
source_quote: prospect-theory conflicts with anchoring (anchoring) when reference-point dependence means the same objective outcome is evaluated differently depending on the arbitrary anchor that sets the gain/loss boundary.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps psychological-safety treat suppressed dissent as a structural signal about the whole organization rather than as isolated interpersonal friction.
source_quote: Systems Thinking
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'necessary for understanding the complexity of human and organizational dynamics' — safety encourages the engagement needed for organizational learning.
activation_condition: When understanding complex organizational dynamics requires an environment where people can surface systemic issues without fear.

source_description: Boundaries help psychological-safety pair openness with clear ownership, standards, and decision rights so candor actually improves the system.
source_quote: ownership, standards, or decision rights
affinity_rationale: Moderate: clear boundaries define the safe space for open dialogue. Not directly in canonical allies but conceptually supports the structure of psychological safety.
activation_condition: When establishing clear behavioral boundaries creates the structured environment needed for safe, open dialogue.

source_description: Critical-thinking turns psychological-safety into a truth-seeking asset by making challenge serve decision quality rather than comfort or status display.
source_quote: candid truth in service of better decisions
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'The ability to engage System 2 (slow, reflective thinking) requires the emotional brain to be managed. Creating space for clear thinking is the prerequisite for critical thought.'
activation_condition: When a psychologically safe environment enables the slow, reflective thinking necessary for rigorous critical analysis.

source_description: Authority-bias suppresses the upward challenge that psychological-safety exists to make possible.
source_quote: formal status discourages upward challenge despite visible risk
affinity_rationale: Canonical tension: 'formal status discourages upward challenge despite visible risk' — authority hierarchy directly undermines psychological safety.
activation_condition: When formal authority status discourages team members from challenging decisions or surfacing risks they observe.

source_description: Confirmation-bias grows when teams suppress dissent and filter evidence to match the prevailing leadership view instead of surfacing contradictions.
source_quote: absence of safety causes teams to suppress dissent and filter evidence
affinity_rationale: Canonical tension: 'absence of safety causes teams to suppress dissent and filter evidence to match the prevailing leadership view.'
activation_condition: When unsafe environments lead teams to suppress contradicting evidence and conform to the prevailing leadership hypothesis.

source_description: Psychological-safety conflicts with authority-bias when formal status discourages upward challenge despite visible risk.
source_quote: psychological-safety vs authority-bias: psychological-safety conflicts with authority-bias (authority bias) when formal status discourages upward challenge despite visible risk.

source_description: Psychological-safety conflicts with confirmation-bias when absence of safety causes teams to suppress dissent and filter evidence to match the prevailing leadership view.
source_quote: psychological-safety vs confirmation-bias: psychological-safety conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when absence of safety causes teams to suppress dissent and filter evidence to match the prevailing leadership view.

source_description: Critical-thinking helps rationalization detection by challenging whether the stated reasons are evidence-backed or merely respectable cover stories.
source_quote: the cure is to inspect the reasoning, not just admire the explanation
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Rational analysis is essential to critical thinking. Critical thinking involves evaluating evidence, noticing bias, and forming judgments based on rational analysis.'
activation_condition: When rational analysis is needed to evaluate evidence and separate facts from personal belief.

source_description: First-principles-thinking helps rationalization strip away inherited story glue and return to what is actually true and causally relevant.
source_quote: return to what is actually true before rebuilding the explanation
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Rational thought is the mechanism for applying First Principles. It strips away assumptions to reveal only the set of facts and the desired outcome.'
activation_condition: When rigorous rational reasoning is needed to strip away assumptions and build conclusions from foundational facts.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps rationalization by showing the broader forces and incentives that a self-protective story tends to erase.
source_quote: story defense often hides the larger system producing the behavior
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Rationality is crucial for designing and diagnosing systems' — understanding causality and how parts interact requires rational, logical thought.
activation_condition: When designing or diagnosing systems requires rational analysis of how parts interact and what emerges from their interactions.

source_description: Confirmation-bias powers rationalization by selecting the evidence and narrative fragments that keep the desired conclusion intact.
source_quote: the mind defends the conclusion instead of reopening it
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'The psychological drive to defend existing models or decisions (rationalization) strongly contributes to confirmation bias' and cognitive entrenchment.
activation_condition: When post-hoc rationalization of decisions drives selective information seeking that confirms rather than challenges existing beliefs.

source_description: Rationalization conflicts with intellectual-humility when post-hoc justification masquerades as reasoned analysis, preventing the genuine self-correction that humility enables.
source_quote: rationalization conflicts with intellectual-humility (intellectual humility) when post-hoc justification masquerades as reasoned analysis, preventing the genuine self-correction that humility enables.

source_description: Rationalization conflicts with first-principles-thinking when rationalization constructs plausible-sounding premises that resist decomposition back to fundamental facts.
source_quote: rationalization conflicts with first-principles-thinking (first principles thinking) when rationalization constructs plausible-sounding premises that resist decomposition back to fundamental facts.

source_description: Cognitive Load Theory helps the router choose simpler structures so complexity is processed without overload.
source_quote: By routing complex ideas through simple organizational structures, concept maps, or analogies, the RMR ensures that new information is broken into absorbable chunks.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'The RMR's goal is to minimize unnecessary cognitive load' — routes complex ideas through simple structures to maximize learning efficiency.
activation_condition: When routing reasoning through appropriate modes requires managing cognitive load to avoid overwhelming working memory.

source_description: Metacognitive reflection gives the router the self-monitoring needed to test whether the current mode is actually working.
source_quote: Metacognition includes the planning aspect ("What are my goals?") and the monitoring aspect ("Could I learn this idea in a different way?").
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'This is the self-awareness required for effective routing' — metacognition enables planning ('What are my goals?') and monitoring ('Could I learn this differently?').
activation_condition: When conscious awareness of one's own reasoning process is needed to select the most effective reasoning mode.

source_description: Confirmation bias hijacks routing by pushing the problem-solving path toward defending a preferred conclusion.
source_quote: This bypasses objective mode selection, forcing the problem-solving effort to defend a preconceived idea.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Motivated reasoning actively hijacks the RMR, forcing the problem-solving effort to defend a preconceived idea regardless of contradictory evidence.'
activation_condition: When motivated reasoning hijacks mode selection to defend preconceived conclusions rather than genuinely discriminating problem structure.

source_description: Commitment and consistency bias blocks route correction even when new evidence indicates another reasoning mode is needed.
source_quote: This desire to avoid the "rigors of thinking" or the appearance of being inconsistent suppresses the RMR’s function to seek truth over comfort.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'creates discomfort with inconsistency, leading people to stick with initial interpretations... suppresses the RMR's function to seek truth over comfort.'
activation_condition: When the desire to appear consistent suppresses route correction even when new evidence indicates a different reasoning mode is needed.

source_description: Reasoning-mode-router conflicts with representativeness-heuristic when familiar labels force premature mode routing.
source_quote: reasoning-mode-router conflicts with representativeness-heuristic when familiar labels force premature mode routing.

source_description: Reasoning-mode-router conflicts with anchoring when the first framing locks route selection despite contradictory evidence.
source_quote: reasoning-mode-router conflicts with anchoring when first framing locks route selection despite contradictory evidence.

source_description: Reasoning-mode-router conflicts with confirmation-bias when the router is tuned to validate a preferred mode instead of the real problem structure.
source_quote: reasoning-mode-router conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when routers are tuned to confirm preferred modes rather than genuinely discriminating problem structure.

source_description: Reasoning-mode-router conflicts with commitment-bias when discomfort with inconsistency suppresses route correction.
source_quote: reasoning-mode-router conflicts with commitment-bias (commitment and consistency bias) when the discomfort of appearing inconsistent suppresses route correction even when new evidence indicates a different reasoning mode is needed.

source_description: Liking-principle helps reciprocity-principle build durable goodwill when mutual giving is paired with positive relationship energy rather than bare obligation.
source_quote: The principle of reciprocation is closely allied with the principles of liking and unity.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'closely allied with the principles of liking and unity. All three are foundational in Cialdini's Core Motives Model of Social Influence.'
activation_condition: When cultivating positive relationships requires leveraging mutual reciprocity alongside liking and unity principles.

source_description: Psychological-safety helps reciprocity-principle become sincere mutual engagement rather than one-way influence by making honest exchange easier.
source_quote: this act dramatically changes the conversational dynamic, demonstrating to the talker that the listener cares enough about the relationship and the issue to work hard to understand what is important to them.
affinity_rationale: Medium confidence: reciprocity requires authentic engagement and trust; not directly in canonical allies but conceptually supportive.
activation_condition: When a psychologically safe environment enables genuine reciprocal exchanges rather than compliance-driven transactions.

source_description: Critical-thinking resists reciprocity-principle when social obligation is pushing assent before evidence and trade-offs have been judged.
source_quote: Critical thinking involves questioning one's own biases, evaluating evidence, and tolerating ambiguity. Since reciprocation can cause assent without thinking, it directly opposes the core critical thinking task
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Since reciprocation can cause assent without thinking, it directly opposes the core critical thinking task of rendering a verdict based on evaluated evidence.'
activation_condition: When the automatic pressure to reciprocate overrides independent evaluation of an offer's actual merit.

source_description: Incentives checks reciprocity-principle when the exchange risks becoming a disguised attempt to buy compliance instead of solving the real problem.
source_quote: Danger when gifts, favors, or access become a distraction from the substance, economics, or ethics of the underlying decision.
affinity_rationale: Medium confidence: curated tension — 'implicit reciprocity norms create obligations that distort the explicit incentive structure designed to align behavior.'
activation_condition: When unspoken reciprocity obligations create shadow incentives that conflict with the formally designed incentive system.

source_description: Reciprocity-principle conflicts with critical-thinking when obligation pressure pushes people toward yes before they have actually judged the merits.
source_quote: Danger when reciprocity pressure creates obligation without informed evaluation, pushing people toward "yes" before they have actually judged the merits.

source_description: Feedback-loops help red-queen-effect turn constant environmental pressure into iterative learning instead of blind acceleration.
source_quote: Implementing Continuous Feedback Loops
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Systems thinking focuses on understanding how parts interact and how feedback loops enable refinement. This ensures that the continuous effort is directed toward effective learning.'
activation_condition: When continuous competitive adaptation requires systemic feedback mechanisms to direct learning and course correction.

source_description: First-principles-thinking helps red-queen-effect escape stale incrementalism when conventional responses no longer keep pace with a changing baseline.
source_quote: First Principles strips away the assumptions associated with conventional methods, allowing the thinker to forge a truly novel, non-obvious solution.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'When RQE forces constant running, incremental changes often fail. First Principles strips away assumptions, allowing the thinker to forge a truly novel, non-obvious solution.'
activation_condition: When incremental competitive responses are insufficient and a breakthrough requires stripping assumptions to find a fundamentally novel solution.

source_description: Confirmation-bias blocks red-queen-effect by making teams defend the old model instead of adapting to disconfirming competitive evidence.
source_quote: Confirmation bias is the instinct to process information to get a conclusion we want rather than what is true, leading to model-defending behavior.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Confirmation bias is the instinct to process information to get a conclusion we *want*... leading to model-defending behavior' — blocks necessary adaptation.
activation_condition: When the drive to confirm existing strategies prevents recognizing that competitive dynamics demand radical adaptation.

source_description: Margin-of-safety resists red-queen-effect when speed pressure would otherwise push adaptation below acceptable downside buffers.
source_quote: Red Queen Effect conflicts with Margin Of Safety when speed pressure and continuous competitive response reduce downside buffers below acceptable thresholds.
affinity_rationale: Canonical tension: 'speed pressure and continuous competitive response reduce downside buffers below acceptable thresholds.'
activation_condition: When competitive urgency to keep pace consumes the safety reserves needed to absorb disruption or failure.

source_description: Red-queen-effect conflicts with margin-of-safety when speed pressure and continuous competitive response reduce downside buffers below acceptable thresholds.
source_quote: Red Queen Effect conflicts with Margin Of Safety when speed pressure and continuous competitive response reduce downside buffers below acceptable thresholds.

source_description: Margin-of-safety complements redundancy by turning spare capacity and backup paths into practical resilience.
source_quote: Systemic and Strategic Redundancy ensures that if one path, asset, or plan fails, others can take over.
affinity_rationale: Strong conceptual link: both models build safety buffers against failure. Redundancy provides backup capacity that margin-of-safety preserves as reserves.
activation_condition: When system resilience requires both backup components (redundancy) and conservative safety buffers (margin of safety).

source_description: Cognitive-load-theory opposes redundancy when duplicated information raises working-memory burden instead of improving learning.
source_quote: Redundancy directly conflicts with the goal of reducing Cognitive Load.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Redundancy directly conflicts with the goal of reducing Cognitive Load' — duplicated information increases mental processing burden and reduces learning effectiveness.
activation_condition: When redundant information or systems increase cognitive processing burden beyond the brain's limited working memory capacity.

source_description: Redundancy conflicts with simplification when building redundant safeguards adds system complexity that itself becomes a new failure source.
source_quote: redundancy conflicts with simplification (simplification) when building redundant safeguards adds system complexity that itself becomes a new failure source.

source_description: Redundancy conflicts with optimization-theory when redundancy preserves slack that optimization would eliminate as waste.
source_quote: redundancy conflicts with optimization-theory (optimization theory) when redundancy preserves slack that optimization would eliminate as waste.

source_description: Systems-thinking strengthens reframing-perspective by redirecting attention from surface faults to the interrelationships creating the problem.
source_quote: Systems Thinking suggests reframing a broken element from a static fault to a damaged system.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'The ability to see complex systems hinges on reframing' — reframing broken elements from static faults to damaged systems forces attention on interrelationships.
activation_condition: When understanding a complex system requires reframing individual faults as systemic dynamics to reveal underlying structure.

source_description: Trade-offs help reframing-perspective expose what different lenses make newly visible about success criteria, downside, and reversibility.
source_quote: Best when the recommendation should actually change what counts as success, what variables matter, or what trade-offs become visible.
affinity_rationale: Moderate: reframing reveals different trade-off structures depending on the lens applied. Not directly in canonical allies but conceptually supports perspective evaluation.
activation_condition: When reframing a problem reveals different trade-off dimensions that were invisible under the original frame.

source_description: Commitment-bias resists reframing-perspective by making teams cling to the old interpretation rather than adopt a better lens.
source_quote: Humans have a strong pressure to remain consistent with previous commitments or interpretations.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'strong, often automatic, pressure to remain consistent with previous commitments conflicts with the flexibility required to discard an ineffective frame and adopt a new one.'
activation_condition: When commitment to a prior interpretation or frame prevents the flexibility needed to adopt a more effective perspective.

source_description: Reframing-perspective conflicts with commitment-bias when commitment to a prior frame makes reframing feel like inconsistency rather than adaptation.
source_quote: reframing-perspective conflicts with commitment-bias (commitment bias) when commitment to a prior frame makes reframing feel like inconsistency rather than adaptation.

source_description: Reframing-perspective conflicts with confirmation-bias when the new frame is selected to confirm a preferred conclusion rather than reveal genuinely alternative structure.
source_quote: reframing-perspective conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when the new frame is selected to confirm a preferred conclusion rather than reveal genuinely alternative structure.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps regression-to-the-mean distinguish ordinary reversion from a real structural change in the system.
source_quote: Systems thinking teaches us that common system structures produce characteristic patterns of behavior.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'RTM is a core concept in seeing patterns of change. Systems thinking teaches us that common system structures produce characteristic patterns of behavior.'
activation_condition: When understanding performance patterns requires recognizing RTM as a self-correcting feedback loop within the broader system.

source_description: Probabilistic-thinking gives regression-to-the-mean the prior and base-rate discipline needed to treat extremes as less likely to persist.
source_quote: Bayesian statistics (probabilistic thinking) provides the mathematical discipline to fight inherent biases and integrate new data with existing background knowledge (the prior probability or base rate).
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Bayesian statistics provides the mathematical discipline to fight inherent biases. RTM provides crucial prior knowledge: extreme results are less probable than results near the mean.'
activation_condition: When probability-based reasoning is needed to correctly interpret extreme outcomes as likely regression rather than persistent signal.

source_description: Confirmation-bias makes regression-to-the-mean look false because observers prefer the treatment story to the reversion story.
source_quote: confirmation bias will cause them to attribute the change to the reprimand, ignoring the RTM mechanism.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'individuals seek or interpret information that affirms their existing beliefs' — e.g., attributing improved performance to a reprimand rather than recognizing natural regression.
activation_condition: When observers attribute natural regression toward the mean to their own interventions because it confirms their preferred causal narrative.

source_description: Regression-to-the-mean conflicts with representativeness-heuristic when extreme streaks are misread as persistent signal.
source_quote: regression-to-the-mean vs representativeness-heuristic: regression-to-the-mean conflicts with representativeness-heuristic (representativeness bias) when extreme streaks are misread as persistent signal.

source_description: Regression-to-the-mean conflicts with survivorship-bias when only visible winners are used to infer stable exceptional performance.
source_quote: regression-to-the-mean vs survivorship-bias: regression-to-the-mean conflicts with survivorship-bias (survivorship bias) when only visible winners are used to infer stable exceptional performance.

source_description: Regression-to-the-mean conflicts with confirmation-bias when observers attribute reversion to treatment effects because regression toward the mean contradicts the preferred causal story.
source_quote: regression-to-the-mean vs confirmation-bias: regression-to-the-mean conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when observers attribute reversion to treatment effects because regression toward the mean contradicts the preferred causal story.

source_description: Premortem complements regret-theory by turning prospective hindsight into a structured review of what could go wrong before commitment.
source_quote: RMF functions as an emotionally driven premortem
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'RMF functions as an emotionally driven premortem — uses mental time travel to picture future failure/regret and looks back to analyze what caused the outcome.'
activation_condition: When anticipating potential regret requires prospective hindsight — imagining the future failure and diagnosing its causes before committing.

source_description: Optionality strengthens regret-theory by checking whether action can be made more reversible before anticipated regret hardens the choice.
source_quote: If the decision is reversible, there is nothing to lose and only information and speed to gain
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'If the RMF indicates action, it is powerful to consider if the decision is reversible. If reversible, there is nothing to lose and only information and speed to gain.'
activation_condition: When regret minimization favors reversible decisions that preserve future options while avoiding the regret of inaction.

source_description: Confirmation-bias is countered by regret-theory when the framework forces the decision maker to consider the regretted path of inaction instead of only the comfortable default story.
source_quote: helps fight the confirmation bias that typically favors the comfortable status quo
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'By forcing the decision maker to consider the outcome of *inaction*, the RMF helps fight confirmation bias that typically favors the comfortable status quo.'
activation_condition: When comfort with the status quo prevents honestly evaluating the regret of inaction alongside the regret of action.

source_description: Regret-theory conflicts with probabilistic-thinking when anticipated regret distorts probability weighting toward vivid worst-case scenarios.
source_quote: regret-theory conflicts with probabilistic-thinking (probabilistic thinking) when anticipated regret distorts probability weighting toward vivid worst-case scenarios.

source_description: Second-order-thinking strengthens regulatory-horizon-scanning by pushing the team to visualize downstream dominoes before rules fully arrive.
source_quote: visualizing all the dominoes
affinity_rationale: RHS is fundamentally about anticipating second-order regulatory effects. Canonical: 'foresee how a decision at one point will ramify to create a distant effect' — core to horizon scanning.
activation_condition: When regulatory foresight requires anticipating the downstream consequences and cascading effects of emerging policy changes.

source_description: Systems-thinking strengthens regulatory-horizon-scanning by converting static regulation snapshots into changing interdependent system patterns.
source_quote: seeing interrelationships and patterns of change rather than static snapshots
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'RHS is dependent on Systems Thinking, which provides the high-level competence model necessary to maintain a big-picture view, identify pattern recognition, and visualize system dynamics.'
activation_condition: When understanding regulatory impact requires mapping how system components interact and what emerges from regulatory changes.

source_description: Confirmation-bias weakens regulatory-horizon-scanning when teams collect policy signals mainly to support the roadmap they already prefer.
source_quote: Confirmation Bias and Motivated Reasoning
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Confirmation bias actively undermines the objectivity required for effective RHS' — model-defending behavior blocks recognition of emerging regulatory signals.
activation_condition: When existing strategic assumptions filter out emerging regulatory signals that contradict the organization's preferred trajectory.

source_description: Regulatory-horizon-scanning conflicts with status-quo-bias when organizations defer compliance adaptation until forced by enforcement.
source_quote: regulatory-horizon-scanning vs status-quo-bias: organizations defer compliance adaptation until forced by enforcement.

source_description: Regulatory-horizon-scanning conflicts with commitment-bias when sunk roadmap choices block timely policy pivot.
source_quote: regulatory-horizon-scanning vs commitment-bias: sunk roadmap choices block timely policy pivot.

source_description: Confirmation-bias strengthens representativeness-heuristic by reinforcing the first stereotype or prototype match and filtering out disconfirming evidence.
source_quote: Representativeness often provides the initial, favored hypothesis or frame. Confirmation bias then reinforces this choice.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Representativeness often provides the initial, favored hypothesis. Confirmation bias then reinforces this choice, causing individuals to seek confirming evidence.'
activation_condition: When an initial similarity-based judgment is reinforced by selective evidence seeking that confirms the representative categorization.

source_description: Narratives makes representativeness-heuristic more seductive when a vivid story feels more diagnostic than the actual distribution of outcomes.
source_quote: The fundamental essence of this mental model lies in the brain's preference for salient narratives over statistical realities.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Representativeness is fueled by WYSIATI and narrative coherence. A vivid story feels more diagnostic than the actual distribution of outcomes.'
activation_condition: When story coherence and vividness make a scenario feel more probable than its actual base rate warrants.

source_description: Base-rates directly counter representativeness-heuristic by forcing denominator discipline when vivid resemblance is doing the work of judgment.
source_quote: Reference Class Forecasting uses historical outcomes from similar problems to predict the current outcome, thereby incorporating statistical reality.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'RH, by definition, ignores critical components of rational probability, such as the base rate and statistical laws' — definitional opposition.
activation_condition: When resemblance-based categorization overrides the actual statistical frequency of events in the reference class.

source_description: Bayesian updating resists representativeness-heuristic by revising on evidence instead of trusting intuitive category fit.
source_quote: Mitigating the risks of RH involves deliberately engaging System 2 thinking to police the quick judgments of System 1.
affinity_rationale: Bayesian reasoning requires incorporating prior probabilities and updating with evidence — directly corrects the representativeness heuristic's neglect of base rates.
activation_condition: When proper Bayesian updating would correct the intuitive probability estimate distorted by surface similarity.

source_description: Critical-thinking opposes representativeness-heuristic by slowing the jump from resemblance to belief and demanding verification of missing evidence.
source_quote: System 2 can be trained to think statistically and logically, enabling it to incorporate base rates and overcome the bias of representativeness.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Critical thinking requires questioning one's mental models and actively seeking evidence that challenges them. RH promotes the opposite: defaulting to the familiar and confirmatory.'
activation_condition: When deliberate critical analysis would reveal that the representative categorization is based on surface resemblance rather than structural evidence.

source_description: Representativeness-heuristic conflicts with base-rates when vivid similarity overrides reference-class frequency and outside-view distribution evidence.
source_quote: representativeness-heuristic conflicts with base-rates (base rates) when vivid similarity overrides reference-class frequency.

source_description: Representativeness-heuristic conflicts with regression-to-the-mean when extreme outcomes are projected as persistent.
source_quote: representativeness-heuristic conflicts with regression-to-the-mean (regression to the mean) when extreme outcomes are projected as persistent.

source_description: Representativeness-heuristic conflicts with narratives when narrative coherence makes a representative-seeming scenario feel more probable than its base rate warrants.
source_quote: representativeness-bias conflicts with narratives (narratives) when narrative coherence makes a representative-seeming scenario feel more probable than its base rate warrants.

source_description: Circle-of-control sharpens resilience by keeping effort on factors within reach instead of spreading attention across uncontrollable stressors.
source_quote: Limiting concerns and efforts only to factors within one's control.
affinity_rationale: Moderate: canonical risks mention 'limiting efforts and concerns only to factors that they have control over' — focusing control scope strengthens resilience.
activation_condition: When resilience requires focusing energy only on factors within one's control rather than dissipating effort on uncontrollable circumstances.

source_description: Scientific-method-evidence-testing helps resilience learn from stress by treating actions as hypotheses to test rather than as identity commitments.
source_quote: By framing actions as hypotheses to be tested and actively seeking to confirm or disprove them, resilience is built into the strategic process.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'By framing actions as hypotheses to be tested and actively seeking to confirm or disprove them, resilience is built into the strategic process.'
activation_condition: When building resilience through iterative hypothesis testing that enables rapid adaptation and learning from failure.

source_description: Root-cause-analysis strengthens resilience by moving recovery from symptom management toward redesign of the stress-producing system.
source_quote: the entire machine can undergo an evolutionary process—continually encountering problems, diagnosing them, designing changes, and implementing them
affinity_rationale: Moderate: understanding root causes of failure strengthens resilience by enabling targeted recovery rather than surface-level patching.
activation_condition: When resilient recovery requires diagnosing the underlying cause of disruption rather than treating symptoms.

source_description: Confirmation-bias blocks resilience because adaptation requires learning from stress signals rather than defending the current recovery story.
source_quote: Confirmation Bias prevents the objective analysis necessary for resilience by causing individuals to seek information that confirms pre-existing beliefs.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Confirmation Bias prevents the objective analysis necessary for resilience by causing individuals to seek information that confirms pre-existing beliefs' — primary enemy of adaptation.
activation_condition: When existing beliefs prevent the objective analysis needed to recognize and adapt to changing circumstances.

source_description: Status-quo-bias fights resilience when recovery is defined as returning to the old state instead of reconfiguring to fit the changed environment.
source_quote: recovery from disruption defaults to restoring pre-crisis conditions rather than pursuing the strategic reconfiguration that the changed environment demands
affinity_rationale: Curated tension: 'recovery from disruption defaults to restoring pre-crisis conditions rather than pursuing the strategic reconfiguration that the changed environment demands.'
activation_condition: When recovery efforts aim to restore the pre-crisis state rather than adapting to the new reality that the disruption revealed.

source_description: Resilience conflicts with growth-mindset when persistence under stress is mistaken for learning progression without capability update.
source_quote: resilience vs growth-mindset: resilience conflicts with growth-mindset (growth mindset) when persistence under stress is mistaken for learning progression without capability update.

source_description: Resilience conflicts with adaptation when recovery routines stabilize the current system but block necessary strategic change.
source_quote: resilience vs adaptation: resilience conflicts with adaptation (adaptation) when recovery routines stabilize the current system but block necessary strategic change.

source_description: Resilience conflicts with confirmation-bias when stress tests are designed to confirm existing recovery plans rather than expose genuine failure modes.
source_quote: resilience vs confirmation-bias: resilience conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when stress tests are designed to confirm existing recovery plans rather than expose genuine failure modes that the plan cannot handle.

source_description: Resilience conflicts with status-quo-bias when recovery from disruption defaults to restoring pre-crisis conditions rather than strategic reconfiguration.
source_quote: resilience vs status-quo-bias: resilience conflicts with status-quo-bias (status quo bias) when recovery from disruption defaults to restoring pre-crisis conditions rather than pursuing the strategic reconfiguration that the changed environment demands.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps risk-assessment see interdependencies and unknown-unknowns before the downside is misread as a set of isolated variables.
source_quote: integrated ecosystem
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Risk assessment is fundamentally supported by systems thinking, which enables the perception of the system as a whole, rather than isolated facts.'
activation_condition: When risk assessment requires viewing the business as an integrated ecosystem to uncover interdependencies and unknown unknowns.

source_description: Constraints help risk-assessment identify the bottlenecks and exposure limits that most shape downside behavior under pressure.
source_quote: focus on the wrong variables
affinity_rationale: Moderate: understanding constraints shapes the boundary conditions within which risks must be assessed. Not directly in canonical allies.
activation_condition: When risk assessment must account for constraints that define the boundaries and limitations of possible mitigation strategies.

source_description: Checklists give risk-assessment a disciplined surface for thresholds, mitigations, and monitoring so key exposures are not left to memory.
source_quote: explicit mitigations, thresholds, and monitoring
affinity_rationale: Moderate: checklists provide systematic coverage of risk factors, reducing the chance of omission. Practical assessment tool.
activation_condition: When systematic enumeration of risk factors is needed to ensure comprehensive coverage and prevent oversight.

source_description: Optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy undermines risk-assessment when downside scenarios are underweighted because the plan feels internally coherent and success feels conjunctively plausible.
source_quote: downside scenarios are underweighted
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'the general tendency to overestimate the probability of conjunctive events leads to unwarranted optimism in evaluating plan success.'
activation_condition: When optimistic assumptions about sequential success probabilities lead to underestimation of overall project risk.

source_description: Representativeness-bias undermines risk-assessment by causing estimators to judge likelihood based on how much a situation resembles a vivid past case rather than on distributional base rates — salient analogies displace the reference-class evidence risk assessment requires.
source_quote: risk-assessment vs representativeness-heuristic: risk-assessment conflicts with representativeness-heuristic (representativeness bias) when salient analogies replace distributional risk estimates.
affinity_rationale: Curated tension: 'salient analogies replace distributional risk estimates' — surface resemblance overrides proper statistical risk quantification.
activation_condition: When vivid analogies to past successes override the distributional analysis needed for accurate risk estimation.

source_description: Risk-assessment conflicts with optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy when downside scenarios are underweighted.
source_quote: risk-assessment vs optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy: risk-assessment conflicts with optimism-bias-and-planning-fallacy (optimism bias and planning fallacy) when downside scenarios are underweighted.

source_description: Risk-assessment conflicts with representativeness-heuristic when salient analogies replace distributional risk estimates — the vivid similarity of a situation to a past case overrides the reference-class base rates that accurate risk quantification requires.
source_quote: risk-assessment vs representativeness-heuristic: risk-assessment conflicts with representativeness-heuristic (representativeness bias) when salient analogies replace distributional risk estimates.

source_description: Systems Thinking helps separate causal structure and scenarios when judging what is bounded risk versus genuine uncertainty.
source_quote: Systems thinking helps break down complexity and assess causality, which is vital for understanding risk.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Systems thinking helps break down complexity and assess causality, vital for understanding risk. Logic trees are powerful tools for disaggregating problems.'
activation_condition: When distinguishing risk from uncertainty requires systemic analysis of interdependencies and causal structures.

source_description: Decomposition supports the model by disaggregating uncertainty into scenarios and assumptions that can be tested separately.
source_quote: Logic trees ... are powerful tools for disaggregating problems into insightful parts, tracking elements for analysis, and modeling different "what if" scenarios.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: logic trees for 'disaggregating problems into insightful parts' — decomposition makes complex risk/uncertainty structures manageable.
activation_condition: When complex risk-uncertainty landscapes need to be broken into tractable components for individual assessment.

source_description: Bloom's Taxonomy reinforces that good uncertainty judgment depends on higher-order evaluation and creation, not shallow recall alone.
source_quote: Evaluation and creation (the top tiers) are essential for generating effective solutions in uncertain domains.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Evaluation and creation (the top tiers) are essential for generating effective solutions in uncertain domains, but depend on solid analytical understanding.'
activation_condition: When assessing uncertainty requires progression through analytical depth levels from understanding to evaluation and creation.

source_description: The Feynman Technique exposes what the operator actually understands and where uncertainty is still being hidden by abstraction.
source_quote: This process of uncovering "what you know versus what you think you know" is crucial for addressing unknown unknowns.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'forces a person to write down an explanation and identify gaps — crucial for addressing unknown unknowns.'
activation_condition: When simplifying complex risk concepts reveals knowledge gaps that distinguish genuine understanding from false confidence.

source_description: Margin of Safety turns the distinction between risk and uncertainty into concrete buffers, thresholds, and reversible commitments.
source_quote: Margin Of Safety turns that distinction into concrete buffers, stop-loss thresholds, and reversible commitments.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'clarifies what can be bounded with evidence and what remains genuinely ambiguous, while Margin Of Safety turns that distinction into concrete buffers. The pairing is especially strong.'
activation_condition: When genuine uncertainty that cannot be resolved before action requires concrete safety buffers, stop-loss thresholds, and reversible commitments.

source_description: Cognitive biases substitute fast intuitive assessment for the slower work needed to classify uncertainty honestly.
source_quote: The model is inherently in conflict with intuitive, fast thinking (System 1), which is responsible for many cognitive errors.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'inherently in conflict with intuitive, fast thinking (System 1), which generates assessments without effort, substituted for methodical System 2 work.'
activation_condition: When automatic cognitive shortcuts distort the careful analysis needed to distinguish measurable risk from genuine uncertainty.

source_description: Confirmation bias pushes the operator to confirm a preferred risk estimate instead of testing whether the distribution is wrong.
source_quote: Managing risk requires finding disconfirming evidence (antithesis), rather than just confirming evidence.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'encourages searching for information that supports an existing belief. Managing risk requires finding disconfirming evidence (antithesis).'
activation_condition: When the desire to resolve uncertainty drives selective evidence gathering that confirms the preferred risk estimate.

source_description: Risk-vs-uncertainty conflicts with confirmation-bias when the desire for closure turns uncertainty into a preferred risk estimate.
source_quote: risk-vs-uncertainty conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when the desire to resolve uncertainty drives selective evidence gathering that confirms the preferred risk estimate rather than genuinely testing whether the distribution is as assumed.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps root-cause-analysis trace how units interact instead of mistaking isolated symptoms for the generative driver.
source_quote: RCA is an integral part of seeing the wholes and the interrelationships rather than just static snapshots.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'RCA is an integral part of seeing the wholes and the interrelationships rather than just static snapshots.'
activation_condition: When root cause diagnosis requires understanding how components interact within the broader system rather than examining failures in isolation.

source_description: Decomposition gives root-cause-analysis the clean cuts it needs to expose the real driver instead of muddled symptom bundles.
source_quote: Any problem of real consequence is too complicated to solve without breaking it down into logical parts.
affinity_rationale: Canonical (via HDPS): the hypothesis guides data gathering and defines required analysis, enabling structured problem disaggregation for RCA.
activation_condition: When complex failure modes need to be decomposed into discrete causal chains for systematic investigation.

source_description: Critical-thinking strengthens root-cause-analysis by taking nothing for granted and probing behind the first acceptable explanation.
source_quote: RCA is fundamentally rooted in the critical thinking attitude of taking nothing for granted.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'RCA is fundamentally rooted in the critical thinking attitude of taking nothing for granted' — probing questions drive deeper insight.
activation_condition: When diagnosing root causes requires objective reasoning that separates facts and logic from personal beliefs and assumptions.

source_description: Correlation-vs-causation is the primary logical error root-cause-analysis is designed to prevent, because it sends diagnosis after associations instead of real drivers.
source_quote: This is the primary logical error RCA seeks to avoid. When the analyst mistakes correlation for causation, they force a relationship where none exists, inevitably causing them to chase the wrong issue and implement ineffective solutions.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'This is the primary logical error RCA seeks to avoid. When the analyst mistakes correlation for causation, they force a relationship where none exists.'
activation_condition: When temporal or spatial association between events is mistaken for a causal relationship, leading to pursuit of the wrong root cause.

source_description: Confirmation-bias corrupts root-cause-analysis by turning diagnosis into defense of the favored story instead of open investigation.
source_quote: RCA demands active openness to new ideas and data and a suspicion of conventional answers.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'the analyst's existing beliefs or organizational culture can exert pressure, especially when a proposal comes from leadership — risks groupthink.'
activation_condition: When organizational pressure or personal beliefs bias the root cause diagnosis toward confirming a preferred explanation.

source_description: Theory-induced-blindness undermines root-cause-analysis when the analyst forces a familiar frame on a novel problem and misses the real driver.
source_quote: Applying RCA frameworks routinely and unquestioningly is dangerous because frameworks reflect mental models with embedded assumptions.
affinity_rationale: Canonical (as Maslow's Hammer): 'compels the analyst to use familiar frameworks even when unhelpful or misleading in a new context — blocks novel solutions.'
activation_condition: When commitment to a familiar diagnostic framework prevents recognizing that the current failure has a genuinely novel causal structure.

source_description: Root-cause-analysis conflicts with simplification when tracing causal chains deeper adds analytical overhead beyond the depth needed for an effective intervention.
source_quote: root-cause-analysis conflicts with simplification (simplification) when tracing causal chains deeper adds analytical overhead beyond the depth needed for an effective intervention.

source_description: Root-cause-analysis conflicts with systems-thinking when single-root-cause framing misses the distributed causality typical of complex adaptive systems.
source_quote: root-cause-analysis conflicts with systems-thinking (systems thinking) when single-root-cause framing misses the distributed causality typical of complex adaptive systems.

source_description: Cognitive-load-theory is the clearest ally for scaffolding-educational because staged support reduces overload and helps schemas form.
source_quote: This is the most crucial ally. Scaffolding is essentially the practical application of CLT.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'This is the most crucial ally. Scaffolding is essentially the practical application of CLT' — ensures learning chunks respect working memory limits.
activation_condition: When educational content must be structured in incremental units that respect the brain's working memory capacity.

source_description: Feynman-technique complements scaffolding-educational by forcing the learner to simplify and restate understanding at each stage.
source_quote: The Feynman technique requires the learner to simplify a concept in plain English and use analogies to fill knowledge gaps.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'Scaffolding is embedded within this learning framework' — Feynman's simplify-and-analogize process is a form of self-scaffolding.
activation_condition: When learning requires simplifying concepts in plain language and using analogies to bridge knowledge gaps as scaffolded steps.

source_description: Expertise-reversal-effect undermines scaffolding-educational by making explicit instructional steps interfere with experts' established schemas, turning support into cognitive friction.
source_quote: The Expertise Reversal Effect: For experts, too many explicit instructions (over-scaffolding) can interfere with their established methods and mental schemas, making the process less efficient than if they had operated freely.
affinity_rationale: Related to Curse of Knowledge: scaffolding designed for novices can become redundant and counterproductive for experts who need less structure.
activation_condition: When scaffolding designed for novices becomes counterproductive for experts whose existing schemas make the structured guidance redundant.

source_description: Scaffolding-educational conflicts with first-principles-thinking when rigid instructional order blocks the learner from independently decomposing the problem.
source_quote: scaffolding-educational conflicts with first-principles-thinking (first principles thinking) when rigid instructional sequence blocks independent decomposition.

source_description: Scaffolding-educational conflicts with step-back when too much instructional structure suppresses reflective reframing.
source_quote: scaffolding-educational conflicts with step-back (step back) when over-structured guidance suppresses reflective reframing.

source_description: Cognitive-load-theory gives scaffolding its operating logic by explaining why chunking and staged support reduce overload during learning.
source_quote: Scaffolding is the implementation mechanism for respecting the limitations described by Cognitive Load Theory.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'This is the most crucial ally. Scaffolding is essentially the practical application of CLT' — reduces cognitive load by breaking complex material into manageable chunks.
activation_condition: When complex material must be broken into incremental chunks to prevent working memory overload during learning or problem-solving.

source_description: Boundaries help scaffolding define what belongs inside the current learning frame so attention stays on the next useful increment.
source_quote: Scaffolding provides an essential structure that establishes boundaries, helping decision-makers focus on what is inside the frame.
affinity_rationale: Moderate: scaffolding provides structural boundaries that define the learning or problem-solving path. Not directly in canonical allies.
activation_condition: When clear boundaries define the scope and sequence of scaffolded steps to prevent scope creep during structured learning.

source_description: System-1 undermines scaffolding by producing snap judgments and substitution bias that bypass the deliberate, structured sequencing scaffolding requires.
source_quote: Scaffolding, as a deliberate, structured process, requires the use of slow thinking (System 2). It counters the tendency of System 1 (instinctual, fast thinking) to produce snap judgments or substitution bias, where a simple, familiar model is used instead of understanding the more complicated reality.
affinity_rationale: Moderate: scaffolding aims to overcome System 1's preference for shortcuts by imposing structured, deliberate steps. Canonical antagonists focus on Curse of Knowledge and Maslow's Hammer.
activation_condition: When the brain's preference for fast, automatic processing resists the deliberate, structured progression that scaffolding requires.

source_description: Scaffolding conflicts with desirable-difficulties when load reduction removes the productive challenge needed for durable learning transfer.
source_quote: scaffolding vs desirable-difficulties: scaffolding conflicts with desirable-difficulties (desirable difficulties) when desirable Difficulties emphasizes challenging learning conditions, contrasting with Scaffolding's focus on reducing cognitive load.

source_description: Scaffolding conflicts with confirmation-bias when the support design reflects the teacher's assumptions instead of diagnosed learner needs.
source_quote: scaffolding vs confirmation-bias: scaffolding conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when scaffold design reflects the instructor's beliefs about what learners need rather than evidence-based diagnosis of actual knowledge gaps.

source_description: Pareto-principle gives scale-economies a concrete way to focus on the small share of activities that truly scale output.
source_quote: Pareto Principle
affinity_rationale: Moderate: scale economies and Pareto principle relate through concentration effects — scaling the critical few inputs. Not directly in canonical allies.
activation_condition: When identifying the high-impact scaling inputs that generate disproportionate returns on the investment in scale.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps scale-economies treat throughput, measurement, and leverage as parts of one designed machine.
source_quote: Systems Thinking
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'The entire concept of scalable efficiency aligns with Systems Thinking — seeing interrelationships rather than things, for seeing patterns of change.'
activation_condition: When scaling requires understanding how business components interact to ensure growth in one area doesn't negatively affect another.

source_description: Confirmation-bias can make scale-economies keep scaling a flawed design because the growth story is emotionally attractive.
source_quote: Confirmation Bias
affinity_rationale: Canonical risks: 'Confirmation Bias/Groupthink — biases can be encoded or reinforced if the team validates their beliefs internally' during scaling.
activation_condition: When internal validation during scaling efforts prevents recognizing that scaling assumptions may not hold in new contexts.

source_description: Scale-economies conflicts with constraints when resource bottlenecks or operational limits prevent the expected leverage from appearing.
source_quote: scale-economies vs constraints

source_description: Scale-economies conflicts with complex-adaptive-systems when dynamic local variation resists the uniformity and predictability that scale wants.
source_quote: scale-economies vs complex-adaptive-systems

source_description: Cognitive-load-theory supports schema-acquisition by reducing working-memory strain so chunking and scaffolding can build stable mental structure.
source_quote: CLT emphasizes optimizing the learning environment to reduce strain on working memory, which frees up capacity for the encoding process necessary for schema formation.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'CLT emphasizes optimizing the learning environment to reduce strain on working memory, which frees up capacity for the encoding process necessary for schema formation.'
activation_condition: When reducing cognitive load is needed to free working memory capacity for the encoding process that builds durable schemas.

source_description: Abstraction helps schema-acquisition compress detail into a usable map without losing the underlying structure people need to retrieve later.
source_quote: Schemas are conceptual maps-deliberately organized, abstracted, and simplified visions of a complex intellectual landscape.
affinity_rationale: Moderate: schemas ARE abstractions — 'deliberately organized, abstracted, and simplified visions of a complex intellectual landscape.' Schema acquisition is the process of building useful abstractions.
activation_condition: When building mental models requires abstracting complex reality into simplified, organized representations that enable pattern recognition.

source_description: Einstellung-effect undermines schema-acquisition by causing the learner to persist with the first recognized pattern instead of encoding a better-fitting schema for the novel situation.
source_quote: Cognitive Entrenchment (The 'Seen This Before' Error): Assuming that a genuinely novel problem is merely a variation of a problem solved previously, causing problem solvers to incorrectly persist in using unhelpful or misleading schemas.
affinity_rationale: Canonical (as Availability Heuristic): 'prevents deliberate schema acquisition by substituting a simpler, known model for the complex actual model needed.'
activation_condition: When familiar schemas are applied reflexively to novel problems, preventing acquisition of more appropriate new schemas.

source_description: Schema-acquisition conflicts with representativeness-heuristic when familiar pattern labels replace context-specific diagnosis.
source_quote: schema-acquisition vs representativeness-heuristic: schema-acquisition conflicts with representativeness-heuristic (representativeness bias) when familiar pattern labels replace context-specific diagnosis.

source_description: Schema-acquisition conflicts with first-principles-thinking when overlearned templates block reconstruction from base facts.
source_quote: schema-acquisition vs first-principles-thinking: schema-acquisition conflicts with first-principles-thinking (first principles thinking) when overlearned templates block reconstruction from base facts.

source_description: Schema-acquisition conflicts with anchoring when the first schema encountered creates a reference point that distorts evaluation of a better-fitting schema.
source_quote: schema-acquisition vs anchoring: schema-acquisition conflicts with anchoring (anchoring) when the first schema encountered creates a reference point that distorts the evaluation of subsequent, potentially better-fitting schemas.

source_description: Systems-thinking helps scientific-method-evidence-testing understand how parts interact before deciding what evidence would actually resolve the issue.
source_quote: SMET builds systemic expertise. It shifts focus from static details to patterns of change by requiring analysis of how individual parts and procedures interact within an underlying system.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'facts alone are unusable unless they hang together on a latticework of theory. SMET provides the mechanism to construct and validate this latticework.'
activation_condition: When evidence testing requires a systemic framework to organize findings into a coherent, validated latticework of theory.

source_description: Experimentation gives scientific-method-evidence-testing a practical way to challenge hypotheses instead of debating them abstractly.
source_quote: The Scientific Method Evidence Testing (SMET) model is fundamentally a strategy for understanding the world through a process of experimentation.
affinity_rationale: SMET IS experimental methodology — hypothesis formation, testing, and revision through structured experimentation is the core activity.
activation_condition: When hypotheses must be rigorously tested through controlled experiments that generate falsifiable evidence.

source_description: Falsifiability keeps scientific-method-evidence-testing honest by requiring disconfirming evidence criteria before the data review starts.
source_quote: Works best when hypotheses can be falsified clearly and the team defines what would count as disconfirming evidence before diving into the data.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'works best when hypotheses can be falsified clearly and the team defines what would count as disconfirming evidence before diving into the data.'
activation_condition: When the quality of evidence testing depends on hypotheses being framed as clearly falsifiable propositions with defined disconfirmation criteria.

source_description: Confirmation-bias is the psychological enemy of scientific-method-evidence-testing because it quietly converts testing into answer defense.
source_quote: scientific-method-evidence-testing conflicts with confirmation-bias (confirmation bias) when disconfirming data is discounted after hypothesis commitment.
affinity_rationale: Canonical: SMET is 'a proven way to address most cognitive biases.' Confirmation bias is the primary obstacle — 'politically protected hypothesis cannot really be falsified.'
activation_condition: When the desire to confirm existing beliefs undermines the genuine falsification that scientific method demands.

source_description: Scientific-method-evidence-testing conflicts with confirmation-bias when disconfirming data is discounted after hypothesis commitment.
